title: TypeScript Introduction
content:
TypeScript Introduction

What is TypeScript?

TypeScript is a strongly typed programming language that builds on JavaScript. It adds static type definitions, making code more readable and debuggable.

Why Use TypeScript?

  • Type Safety: Catch errors at compile time
  • Better IDE Support: Enhanced autocomplete and refactoring
  • Modern Features: Access to latest JavaScript features
  • Better Collaboration: Self-documenting code
TypeScript Installation
npm install -g typescript
tsc --version
tsc filename.ts

title: TypeScript vs JavaScript
content:
TypeScript vs JavaScript

Key Differences

  • Static Typing: TypeScript has type annotations, JavaScript doesn't
  • Compilation: TypeScript needs compilation, JavaScript is interpreted
  • Interfaces: TypeScript has interfaces, JavaScript doesn't
  • Generics: TypeScript supports generics, JavaScript doesn't
JavaScript vs TypeScript
function greet(name) {
  return "Hello, " + name;
}

function greet(name: string): string {
  return "Hello, " + name;
}

TypeScript Compilation Process

TypeScript (.ts) files are compiled to JavaScript (.js) using the TypeScript compiler (tsc).

tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES6",
    "module": "commonjs",
    "strict": true,
    "outDir": "./dist"
  }
}

title: Basic Types
content:
Basic Types

Primitive Types

  • string: Text values
  • number: All numbers (integers, floats)
  • boolean: true/false
  • null: Null value
  • undefined: Undefined value
Basic Type Examples
let name: string = "John";
let age: number = 25;
let price: number = 19.99;
let isActive: boolean = true;
let numbers: number[] = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
let names: Array = ["John", "Jane"];
let anything: any = "hello";
anything = 42;

Special Types

Void and Never
function logMessage(message: string): void {
  console.log(message);
}

function throwError(error: string): never {
  throw new Error(error);
}

title: Type Annotations
content:
Type Annotations

Variable Type Annotations

Type annotations explicitly specify the type of a variable.

Variable Annotations
let username: string = "johndoe";
let userCount: number = 42;
let isLogged: boolean = false;
let inferred = "hello";
let inferredNum = 100;

Function Type Annotations

Function Parameters and Return
function add(a: number, b: number): number {
  return a + b;
}

const multiply = (x: number, y: number): number => x * y;

function greet(name: string, greeting?: string): string {
  return greeting ? greeting + ", " + name + "!" : "Hello, " + name + "!";
}

Default Parameters

Default Parameter
function createUser(name: string, role: string = "user") {
  return { name, role };
}

createUser("John");
createUser("Jane", "admin");

title: Interfaces
content:
Interfaces

What is an Interface?

An interface defines the structure of an object. It specifies what properties and methods an object should have.

Basic Interface
interface User {
  name: string;
  age: number;
  email: string;
  isActive?: boolean;
}

const user: User = {
  name: "John",
  age: 30,
  email: "john@example.com"
};

Interface Methods

Methods in Interface
interface Calculator {
  add(a: number, b: number): number;
  subtract(a: number, b: number): number;
}

const mathOps: Calculator = {
  add: (a, b) => a + b,
  subtract: (a, b) => a - b
};

Readonly Properties

Readonly
interface Point {
  readonly x: number;
  readonly y: number;
}

const point: Point = { x: 10, y: 20 };

title: Classes
content:
Classes in TypeScript

Class Basics

Classes are blueprints for creating objects with properties and methods.

Basic Class
class Person {
  name: string;
  age: number;
  
  constructor(name: string, age: number) {
    this.name = name;
    this.age = age;
  }
  
  greet(): string {
    return "Hello, I am " + this.name;
  }
}

const person = new Person("John", 30);
console.log(person.greet());

Access Modifiers

  • public: Accessible everywhere (default)
  • private: Accessible only within the class
  • protected: Accessible within class and subclasses
  • readonly: Can only be assigned once
Access Modifiers
class BankAccount {
  public readonly id: string;
  private balance: number;
  protected name: string;
  
  constructor(id: string, initialBalance: number) {
    this.id = id;
    this.balance = initialBalance;
    this.name = "Account";
  }
  
  public deposit(amount: number): void {
    this.balance += amount;
  }
  
  private calculateInterest(): number {
    return this.balance * 0.05;
  }
}

title: Inheritance
content:
Inheritance

What is Inheritance?

Inheritance allows a class to inherit properties and methods from another class.

Class Inheritance
class Animal {
  name: string;
  
  constructor(name: string) {
    this.name = name;
  }
  
  makeSound(): void {
    console.log("Some sound");
  }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
  breed: string;
  
  constructor(name: string, breed: string) {
    super(name);
    this.breed = breed;
  }
  
  makeSound(): void {
    console.log("Woof! Woof!");
  }
}

const dog = new Dog("Buddy", "Labrador");
dog.makeSound();

Super Keyword

Use super to call parent class methods and constructors.

Using Super
class Cat extends Animal {
  constructor(name: string) {
    super(name);
  }
  
  makeSound(): void {
    super.makeSound();
    console.log("Meow!");
  }
}

Abstract Classes

Abstract Class
abstract class Shape {
  abstract area(): number;
  
  display(): void {
    console.log("This is a shape");
  }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
  radius: number;
  
  constructor(radius: number) {
    super();
    this.radius = radius;
  }
  
  area(): number {
    return Math.PI * this.radius * this.radius;
  }
}

title: Generics
content:
Generics

What are Generics?

Generics allow you to create reusable components that work with multiple types instead of a single type.

Generic Function
function identity(arg: T): T {
  return arg;
}

const num = identity(5);
const str = identity("hello");
const auto = identity(10);

Generic Interfaces

Generic Interface
interface KeyValuePair {
  key: K;
  value: V;
}

const pair1: KeyValuePair = {
  key: "age",
  value: 25
};

const pair2: KeyValuePair = {
  key: 1,
  value: "one"
};

Generic Classes

Generic Class
class Box {
  content: T;
  
  constructor(content: T) {
    this.content = content;
  }
  
  getContent(): T {
    return this.content;
  }
}

const stringBox = new Box("Hello");
const numberBox = new Box(42);

title: Type Aliases
content:
Type Aliases

What are Type Aliases?

Type aliases create a new name for a type. They help simplify complex type definitions.

Basic Type Alias
type StringOrNumber = string | number;

type User = {
  name: string;
  age: number;
  email: string;
};

const user: User = {
  name: "John",
  age: 30,
  email: "john@example.com"
};

Union Types

Union Types
type Status = "pending" | "approved" | "rejected";

let orderStatus: Status = "pending";
orderStatus = "approved";

function format(value: string | number): string {
  if (typeof value === "string") {
    return value.toUpperCase();
  }
  return value.toFixed(2);
}

Intersection Types

Intersection Types
interface Person {
  name: string;
}

interface Employee {
  employeeId: number;
  department: string;
}

type EmployeePerson = Person & Employee;

const emp: EmployeePerson = {
  name: "John",
  employeeId: 12345,
  department: "Engineering"
};

title: Enums
content:
Enums

What are Enums?

Enums allow you to define a set of named constants. They make code more readable and type-safe.

Numeric Enum
enum Direction {
  Up,
  Down,
  Left,
  Right
}

console.log(Direction.Up);
console.log(Direction["Up"]);

enum StatusCode {
  Success = 200,
  NotFound = 404,
  Error = 500
}

String Enums

String Enum
enum LogLevel {
  ERROR = "ERROR",
  WARN = "WARN",
  INFO = "INFO",
  DEBUG = "DEBUG"
}

function log(message: string, level: LogLevel): void {
  console.log("[" + level + "] " + message);
}

log("Something went wrong", LogLevel.ERROR);

Const Enums

Const Enum
const enum Color {
  Red,
  Green,
  Blue
}

const color = Color.Red;

title: Type Guards
content:
Type Guards

What are Type Guards?

Type guards narrow down the type of a variable within a conditional block.

typeof Type Guard
function processValue(value: string | number): string {
  if (typeof value === "string") {
    return value.toUpperCase();
  } else {
    return value.toFixed(2);
  }
}

instanceof Guard

instanceof Type Guard
class Dog {
  bark(): void {
    console.log("Woof!");
  }
}

class Cat {
  meow(): void {
    console.log("Meow!");
  }
}

function makeSound(animal: Dog | Cat): void {
  if (animal instanceof Dog) {
    animal.bark();
  } else {
    animal.meow();
  }
}

Custom Type Guards

Custom Type Guard
interface Fish {
  swim(): void;
}

interface Bird {
  fly(): void;
}

function isFish(animal: Fish | Bird): animal is Fish {
  return (animal as Fish).swim !== undefined;
}

function move(animal: Fish | Bird): void {
  if (isFish(animal)) {
    animal.swim();
  } else {
    animal.fly();
  }
}

title: Utility Types
content:
Utility Types

Built-in Utility Types

TypeScript provides several utility types to facilitate common type transformations.

Partial and Required

Partial and Required
interface User {
  name: string;
  age: number;
  email: string;
}

type PartialUser = Partial;
type RequiredUser = Required;

const user1: PartialUser = { name: "John" };
const user2: RequiredUser = { name: "John", age: 30, email: "a@b.com" };

Pick and Omit

Pick and Omit
interface Article {
  id: number;
  title: string;
  content: string;
  author: string;
  createdAt: Date;
}

type ArticlePreview = Pick;
type ArticleWithoutMeta = Omit;

Record and Readonly

Record and Readonly
type UserRoles = Record;
const roles: UserRoles = {
  admin: "Administrator",
  user: "Regular User"
};

type ImmutableUser = Readonly;
const user: ImmutableUser = { name: "John", age: 30 };

title: Modules
content:
Modules

Export and Import

Modules allow you to organize code into separate files and import them where needed.

Export
export function add(a: number, b: number): number {
  return a + b;
}

export function subtract(a: number, b: number): number {
  return a - b;
}

export default class Calculator {
  multiply(a: number, b: number): number {
    return a * b;
  }
}

Import

Import Syntax
import { add, subtract } from "./math";
import { add as sum } from "./math";
import * as MathUtils from "./math";
import Calculator from "./Calculator";

Re-exporting

Re-export
export { add, subtract } from "./math";
export { default } from "./Calculator";
export * from "./utils";

title: Namespaces
content:
Namespaces

What are Namespaces?

Namespaces are a way to group related code and avoid global scope pollution.

Basic Namespace
namespace Geometry {
  export class Circle {
    constructor(public radius: number) {}
    
    area(): number {
      return Math.PI * this.radius ** 2;
    }
  }
  
  export class Rectangle {
    constructor(public width: number, public height: number) {}
    
    area(): number {
      return this.width * this.height;
    }
  }
}

const circle = new Geometry.Circle(5);
console.log(circle.area());

Modules vs Namespaces

  • Modules: Preferred in modern TypeScript
  • Namespaces: Legacy feature

title: Advanced Types - Mapped Types
content:
Mapped Types

What are Mapped Types?

Mapped types transform existing types into new types by iterating over keys.

Basic Mapped Type
interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
  email: string;
}

type PartialUser = {
  [K in keyof User]?: User[K];
};

type ReadonlyUser = {
  readonly [K in keyof User]: User[K];
};

Key Remapping

Key Remapping
interface Response {
  user_id: number;
  user_name: string;
  user_email: string;
}

type CamelCaseResponse = {
  [K in keyof Response as `user${Capitalize}`]: Response[K];
};

title: Advanced Types - Conditional Types
content:
Conditional Types

What are Conditional Types?

Conditional types select one of two types based on a condition.

Basic Conditional Type
= T>type IsString extends string ? true : false; type A = IsString; type B = IsString;

Infer Keyword

Infer
type ReturnType = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never;

function getData() {
  return { id: 1, name: "John" };
}

type DataType = ReturnType;

type FirstArg = T extends (arg: infer A) => any ? A : never;

Exclude and Extract

Utility Types
type Exclude = T extends U ? never : T;
type Extract = T extends U ? T : never;

type T = Extract<"a" | "b" | "c", "a" | "f">;
type U = Exclude<"a" | "b" | "c", "a">;

title: Decorators
content:
Decorators

What are Decorators?

Decorators are a way to add metadata or modify classes, methods, and properties.

Enable Decorators
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "experimentalDecorators": true,
    "emitDecoratorMetadata": true
  }
}

Class Decorator

Class Decorator
function sealed(constructor: Function) {
  Object.seal(constructor);
  Object.seal(constructor.prototype);
}

@sealed
class Greeter {
  greeting: string;
  
  constructor(message: string) {
    this.greeting = message;
  }
  
  greet(): string {
    return "Hello, " + this.greeting;
  }
}

Method Decorator

Method Decorator
function enumerable(value: boolean) {
  return function (target: any, propertyKey: string, descriptor: PropertyDescriptor) {
    descriptor.enumerable = value;
  };
}

class Calculator {
  @enumerable(false)
  add(a: number, b: number): number {
    return a + b;
  }
}

title: Error Handling
content:
Error Handling

Try-Catch

Handle errors gracefully using try-catch blocks.

Basic Error Handling
function divide(a: number, b: number): number {
  if (b === 0) {
    throw new Error("Cannot divide by zero");
  }
  return a / b;
}

try {
  const result = divide(10, 0);
} catch (error) {
  console.error("Error:", error.message);
} finally {
  console.log("Done");
}

Custom Error Classes

Custom Errors
class ValidationError extends Error {
  constructor(message: string) {
    super(message);
    this.name = "ValidationError";
  }
}

function validateAge(age: number): void {
  if (age < 0) {
    throw new ValidationError("Age cannot be negative");
  }
}

try {
  validateAge(-5);
} catch (error) {
  if (error instanceof ValidationError) {
    console.log("Validation failed:", error.message);
  }
}

Async Error Handling

Async Errors
async function fetchData(): Promise {
  try {
    const response = await fetch("https://api.example.com/data");
    if (!response.ok) {
      throw new Error("Network response was not ok");
    }
    return await response.json();
  } catch (error) {
    console.error("Fetch error:", error);
    throw error;
  }
}

title: TypeScript with React
content:
TypeScript with React

React with TypeScript

TypeScript works seamlessly with React for type-safe component development.

Functional Component
import React from "react";

interface Props {
  name: string;
  age?: number;
  onClick: () => void;
}

const UserCard: React.FC = ({ name, age = 0, onClick }) => {
  return (
    

      
{name}

      
Age: {age}

    

  );
};

useState with Types

useState Types
const [name, setName] = useState("");

interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
}
const [user, setUser] = useState(null);

const [items, setItems] = useState([]);

Event Types

React Event Types
const handleChange = (e: React.ChangeEvent) => {
  setValue(e.target.value);
};

const handleSubmit = (e: React.FormEvent) => {
  e.preventDefault();
};

const handleClick = (e: React.MouseEvent) => {
  console.log("Clicked!");
};

title: TypeScript Best Practices
content:
TypeScript Best Practices

Code Quality Tips

  • Enable strict mode: Catch more errors at compile time
  • Avoid any: Use unknown instead
  • Use type inference: Let TypeScript infer types
  • Prefer interfaces: Use interfaces for object shapes
Strict tsconfig
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true,
    "noImplicitAny": true,
    "strictNullChecks": true,
    "strictFunctionTypes": true,
    "noUnusedLocals": true,
    "noUnusedParameters": true
  }
}

Best Practices Examples

Do and Do Not
interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
}

let data: unknown = getData();
if (typeof data === "string") {
  console.log(data.toUpperCase());
}

Naming Conventions

  • Use PascalCase for interfaces, types, and classes
  • Use camelCase for variables and functions
  • Use UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for constants

title: TypeScript Summary
content:
TypeScript Course Summary

What You Have Learned

Congratulations on completing the TypeScript tutorial!

  • Basics: Types, annotations, interfaces
  • Classes: OOP concepts, inheritance, modifiers
  • Advanced: Generics, utility types, decorators
  • Organization: Modules, namespaces
  • Patterns: Type guards, conditional types

TypeScript Quick Reference

Common Types
let str: string;
let num: number;
let bool: boolean;
let arr: string[];
let arr2: Array;
interface User { name: string; age: number; }
let user: User;
function fn(a: string, b: number): string { return a; }
type A = string | number;
type B = Type1 & Type2;

Next Steps

  • Practice: Build projects with TypeScript
  • Frameworks: Learn React with TypeScript
  • Advanced: Study design patterns

Keep Coding!

TypeScript makes JavaScript development more enjoyable!
